title: Showcases
text:
Presenter(s): Kevin Fraser (PI), Vinuri Mendis (PhD Student), Yantong Tan (PhD Student), Rebecca Asham (MSc Student), Tamara Sturch (MSc Student)
Affiliation(s): University of Manitoba
Organization Type: Academic


The Avian Behaviour and Conservation Lab at the University of Manitoba investigates how birds respond behaviourally to anthropogenic environmental change. Our research explores how birds time their daily and seasonal activities, and how flexibly these schedules respond to new conditions associated with climate change. We also investigate how artificial light at night alters the timing and movement behaviour of birds. Through field-based experiments, from the Neotropics to the sub-Arctic, we study primarily migratory species, including aerial insectivores such as Purple Martin and Eastern Whip-poor-will. We also collaborate with northern Indigenous communities in Manitoba to address shared concerns regarding change in avian behaviour and populations.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
text:
Presenter(s): Kevin Fraser (PI), Vinuri Mendis (PhD Student), Yantong Tan (PhD Student), Rebecca Asham (MSc Student), Tamara Sturch (MSc Student)
Affiliation(s): University of Manitoba
Organization Type: Academic


The Avian Behaviour and Conservation Lab at the University of Manitoba investigates how birds respond behaviourally to anthropogenic environmental change. Our research explores how birds time their daily and seasonal activities, and how flexibly these schedules respond to new conditions associated with climate change. We also investigate how artificial light at night alters the timing and movement behaviour of birds. Through field-based experiments, from the Neotropics to the sub-Arctic, we study primarily migratory species, including aerial insectivores such as Purple Martin and Eastern Whip-poor-will. We also collaborate with northern Indigenous communities in Manitoba to address shared concerns regarding change in avian behaviour and populations.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Banding in the Age of Technology
text:
Banding in the Age of Technology
Presenter(s): Marcel Gahbauer, Lesley Howes, Barbara Frei, Cyndi Smith, Pam Sinclair
Affiliation(s): Canadian Wildlife Service, Canadian Migration Monitoring Network
Organization Type: eNGO

Abstract
Banding and recapture of birds in the early 1900s provided the first documentation of individual bird movements in North America. In recent decades, bird monitoring has been revolutionized by technological advances such as satellite telemetry, geolocators, and automated radio telemetry (e.g., Motus), as well as the growth of ‘big data’ through participatory science, and the use of artificial intelligence for species identification. This has prompted questions about whether traditional methods such as banding have become irrelevant. On the contrary, standardized banding efforts remain fundamental to understanding longevity, annual survival rates, age structure, and physiological responses to environmental change. Banding data also support a wide range of conservation and management outcomes, including disease surveillance and assessment of habitat restoration. Since 1998, the Canadian Migration Monitoring Network has facilitated communication and collaboration among bird observatories across the country. Maintaining strong banding programs while integrating emerging technologies is critical to advancing ornithological research and conservation.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: CanAvian: Advancing Avian Data Integration in Canada
text:
CanAvian: Advancing Avian Data Integration in Canada
Presenter(s): Sofia Guest1, Catherine Jardine2
Affiliation(s): 1. Alberta Biodiversity Monitoring Institute, 2. Birds Canada
Organization Type: eNGO

Abstract
The Canadian Network of Avian Data (CanAvian) unites a broad community of researchers, conservation practitioners, and volunteer scientists who generate, manage, and share extensive biodiversity data needed for ecological research. CanAvian is governed by a Steering Committee, Advisory Committees, and Working Groups coordinating priorities across science, data management, and community engagement. CanAvian brings together three of Canada’s largest biodiversity data platforms: NatureCounts (380 million records), Motus (8 billion records), and WildTrax (16 million records), supporting over 4,500 scientific publications. Supported by the Digital Research Alliance of Canada, CanAvian is establishing harmonized data access policies and interoperability standards to support FAIR and CARE principles and improve data sharing across platforms and data repositories. These connections will support increasingly integrated and large-scale analyses required by modern ecological research and Canada’s biodiversity commitments. CanAvian has open pathways for membership, data contribution, and data access and invites participation from across Canada’s ornithological community.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Delivering Strong Science at Birds Canada
text:
Delivering Strong Science at Birds Canada
Presenter(s): Douglas C. Tozer
Affiliation(s): Director, Waterbirds & Wetlands, Birds Canada
Organization Type: eNGO

Abstract
Birds Canada delivers strong science across the country and beyond through more than a dozen regional networks of employees, volunteers, and partners collaborating toward shared conservation goals. Our science is diverse involving many different research themes, core questions, methodologies, ecosystems, and focal species. Much of our science falls under two key initiatives: tracking the state of Canada’s birds and leveraging science to drive conservation. Our 50+ science staff members leverage data from volunteer scientists and conservation technologies (NatureCounts, Motus, WildTrax, and more) to answer timely questions of conservation interest, and we often partner with universities on graduate student projects. Ultimately all of our science helps generate the critical knowledge and evidence needed to catalyze actions that help birds and their habitats thrive. In this presentation, we give a top-down overview of science at Birds Canada, and describe a broad selection of examples of Birds Canada science in action.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Drivers of Migratory Bird Population Change in Working Landscapes
text:
Drivers of Migratory Bird Population Change in Working Landscapes
Presenter(s): Ann E. McKellar, Nancy A. Mahony, Greg W. Mitchell, Elizabeth A. Gow
Affiliation(s): Environment and Climate Change Canada, Partner institutions
Organization Type: Government

Abstract
Drs Ann McKellar, Nancy Mahony, Greg Mitchell, and Elizabeth Gow are Research Scientists from Environment and Climate Change Canada’s Science and Technology Branch, Biodiversity Research Division, who study the conservation of migratory birds in working landscapes. Their research, which extends from the Canadian western forests and Prairies to Atlantic Canada, takes a full annual cycle approach to 1) examine effects of agriculture, forestry, and urbanization on avian distribution, abundance, and fitness, 2) assess population structure and migratory connectivity of priority species and species at risk, and 3) quantify threats associated with aerial and urban habitats. They aim to develop evidence-based solutions that support biodiversity, ecosystem goods and services, and human well-being in working landscapes. The team, including their graduate students, collaborates extensively with universities (where they are Adjunct professors), ENGOs, other federal departments, and governments, both nationally and internationally.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Ecotoxicology and Conservation of Prairie Bird Communities
text:
Ecotoxicology and Conservation of Prairie Bird Communities
Presenter(s): Christy Morrissey
Affiliation(s): Professor of Biology, University of Saskatchewan
Organization Type: Academic

Abstract
We are an ecotoxicology lab with a strong conservation focus. Our lab studies the effects of environmental contaminants and other non-pollution stressors to better understand how individual birds, populations and communities respond to threats. We use a variety of methods from captive and field controlled dosing studies, beaker and limnocorral studies on aquatic insects that are important prey, and small and big scale field experiments to assess short and long term responses to communities across the Prairie region. A major focus is on how pesticides and other agricultural stressors impact common grassland and wetland associated songbirds through altered prey, neurotoxicity, and disturbance. We also conduct research on the effects of industrial pollution to shorebirds.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Integrative Ornithology at AFAR
text:
Integrative Ornithology at AFAR
Presenter(s): Scott MacDougall-Shackleton, Chris Guglielmo, Carrie Branch, Yolanda Morbey, Beth MacDougall-Shackleton, Alice Boyle
Affiliation(s): Western University
Organization Type: Academic

Abstract
The Advanced Facility for Avian Research at Western University is a world class facility consisting of faculty and trainees that aim to understand how the environment shapes bird ecology. Researchers across disciplines use integrative approaches to investigate everything bird, from neurobiology and behaviour to migratory physiology and conservation. To showcase the innovative research possible at AFAR, we will present on the tools and facilities used to address a multitude of research questions, both ongoing and for the future. In addition to working toward a better understanding of bird biology and ecology, we are passionate about training the next generation of critical thinkers and scientists.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Landscape Change and Avian Ecology at Thompson Rivers University
text:
Landscape Change and Avian Ecology at Thompson Rivers University
Presenter(s): Matt Reudink, Nancy Flood, Stephen Joly, Kara Lefevre
Affiliation(s): Thompson Rivers University
Organization Type: Academic

Abstract
Thompson Rivers University is in the southern interior of British Columbia on the ancestral lands of the Tk’emlúps te Secwépemc (Kamloops main campus) within Secwepemcúl̓ecw, the traditional and unceded territory of the Secwépemc. This vast area of grasslands, forests, mountains, rivers and lakes is larger than all but four European countries, stretching from Jasper to Revelstoke to Kamloops to north of Williams Lake. Its diverse landscapes provide a rich opportunity for research about the impact of accelerating environmental change on avian ecology and to partner with First Nations. This showcase will highlight core themes of research guided by a group of faculty members who collaboratively mentor graduate students on projects that include: conservation of grassland-dependent bird species; braiding Indigenous and Western knowledges to inform species conservation; responses of bird communities to urbanization, industrial activity, and wildfire; and community-engaged efforts to monitor avian population change.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Monitoring Bird Populations in Canada’s National Parks
text:
Monitoring Bird Populations in Canada’s National Parks
Presenter(s): Lucy Patterson
Affiliation(s): Ecological Monitoring Specialist, Parks Canada Agency
Organization Type: Government

Abstract
Parks Canada Agency monitors birds in protected areas across Canada within a common framework called the Ecological Integrity Monitoring Program (EIMP). This program assesses the status and trend of ecological integrity of 119 ecosystems within 43 national parks across the country and can enable prioritization and evaluation of management actions to enhance ecosystem integrity. Birds are one of the most common indicators, being monitored within six different ecosystem types in 39 parks. Of the avian indicators assessed in 2025, the proportion in good, fair and poor condition was 56%, 31%, and 11%, and the proportion improving, stable and declining was 11%, 43% and 19%. We provide a brief overview of the EIMP and a summary of bird indicators within the program. We also present a case study from a protected area to demonstrate how a monitoring indicator can be developed and used in the ecosystem management planning process.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: National Status and Trends of Canada’s Birds
text:
National Status and Trends of Canada’s Birds
Presenter(s): Adam C. Smith, Jackson Kusack, Sarah Gutowsky, Dave Iles, Willow English, Marcel Gahbauer, Michel Gendron, Frederique Tremblay, Marie-Anne Hudson, Christian Roy, Charles Francis
Affiliation(s): Canadian Wildlife Service
Organization Type: Government

Abstract
The Canadian Wildlife Service’s national wildlife management and assessment teams conduct research supporting the population monitoring and management of Canada’s migratory birds. Research centers around three overall themes: 1) optimizing applied population monitoring, for both professional and citizen science programs, as well as automated monitoring technologies; 2) developing integrated statistical methods to improve estimates of population sizes, status, trends and distribution; and 3) coordinating research and reporting with international partners to inform conservation across the full annual cycle. Ongoing and upcoming research projects involve: developing integrated spatiotemporal models of population status and drivers of trends using monitoring, remote sensing, and demographic data; developing object detection models to automate counts from seabird colony photo surveys; improving statistical models for estimating density and population sizes; improving breeding waterfowl population estimates by optimizing survey designs and accounting for bias in sex ratios; and improving surveys and models to estimate harvest of migratory birds.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Ornithological research and conservation at the Ontario Ministry of Natural Resources
text:
Ornithological research and conservation at the Ontario Ministry of Natural Resources
Presenter(s): Glen Brown1, Richard Feldman1, Jeff Bowman1, Rod Brook1, Erica Newton1, Chrystyn Jones (MSc Student)2, Richard Huang (MSc Student)2, Ingrid Tello (PhD Student)3
Affiliation(s): 1. Ontario Ministry of Natural Resources, 2. Trent University, 3. Western University
Organization Type: Government & Academic

Abstract
At the Wildlife Research and Monitoring Section of the Ontario Ministry of Natural Resources, we coordinate three strands of avian research: the breeding ecology of shorebirds and waterfowl in the Hudson Bay Lowlands, the nutrient dynamics behind grassland bird habitat use, and climate effects on Wild Turkey distribution. Glen Brown’s work in Ontario’s Far North - one of the largest contiguous wetland systems in the world - has shown how the region may be vulnerable to the deepening of the permafrost active layer and wetland drying. Richard Feldman’s work in the Carden Alvar ecosystem has shown the connections between plant stoichiometry and variation in the quantity and quality of the prey of Eastern Whip-poor-will. Jeff Bowman’s work has focused on strategies that Ontario’s reintroduced Wild Turkeys have used to succeed in a northern climate. Combined, we contribute to conserving birds across the province, and involving students in applied ecological research.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Partners in Flight: Collaboration from Science to Application
text:
Partners in Flight: Collaboration from Science to Application
Presenter(s): Becky Stewart
Affiliation(s): Wildlife Biologist, Environment and Climate Change Canada
Organization Type: Partnership (Academia, Government, NGO, Industry)

Abstract
Partners In Flight (PIF) is a network of organizations dedicated to landbird conservation. Partners range from not-for-profit organizations to academics, governments, funders, and industry. Each partner brings their strengths, capacities, and expertise to achieve conservation for our shared birds. This presentation will highlight PIF’s science-based, open access data tools such as the Avian Conservation and Assessment Database, a species conservation status assessment tool that evaluates vulnerability factors at continental and regional scales. Other initiatives include revised model for population estimates, tools to evaluate progress in achieving continental population trend objectives and maps to explore migratory connections in the absence of tracking data. Finally, we’ll focus on how these tools support adaptive management and measurable outcomes for birds and how we (including you), as the bird research and conservation community can leverage and amplify these tools and approaches for bird conservation.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Seabird Ecology at the Atlantic Laboratory for Avian Research
text:
Seabird Ecology at the Atlantic Laboratory for Avian Research
Presenter(s): Heather Major (Professor), Amanda Giovinazzo, Maddy Talpt, Clare Taylor, Kayla Cannon, Daniel Oliker
Affiliation(s): University of New Brunswick
Organization Type: Academic

Abstract
Based at the University of New Brunswick’s Saint John Campus, the Atlantic Laboratory for Avian Research is directed by seabird biologist Dr. Heather Major. The lab studies relationships between bird populations and environmental change in marine and island ecosystems. Species of interest include the Atlantic Puffin, Razorbill, Common Murre, Arctic Tern, and Leach’s Storm-petrel primarily studied on Machias Seal Island (MSI) in the Bay of Fundy. MSI has been the focal site for the labs’ long-term seabird monitoring project that has been operated annually since 1995. In addition, the lab is currently collaborating on a project assessing feasibility of an island restoration at Seal Island, NS by removing introduced species.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: The Science that Powers Waterfowl Conservation
text:
The Science that Powers Waterfowl Conservation
Presenter(s): Matt Dyson, James Paterson, Vanessa Harriman, Lauren Bortolotti, Pascal Badiou, Samuel Robinson
Affiliation(s): Ducks Unlimited Canada
Organization Type: eNGO

Abstract
The Institute for Wetland and Waterfowl Research (IWWR) at Ducks Unlimited Canada conducts interdisciplinary research that advances ornithology within the broader context of wetland ecosystems. Centered on waterfowl ecology, IWWR studies waterfowl habitat use, productivity, and population dynamics, with particular emphasis on breeding landscapes such as the Prairie Pothole Region, while extending across boreal and other wetland systems nationally. Beyond core ornithological questions, IWWR develops quantitative tools and models to guide conservation planning and evaluate program outcomes, ensuring that habitat investments are effective, adaptive, and evidence-based. This includes assessing when programs should be refined, scaled, or phased out. Complementing our work in waterfowl ecology, we have developed substantial research capacity in biodiversity, climate change, carbon dynamics, wetland biogeochemistry, and sustainable agriculture, reflecting emerging priorities in wetland conservation science. Together, these efforts integrate waterfowl science with broader ecological processes to support both understanding and adaptive, outcome-driven conservation.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Twenty Years of Boreal Avian Modelling
text:
Twenty Years of Boreal Avian Modelling
Presenter(s): Elly Knight, Steven Van Wilgenburg, Diana Stralberg, Steve Cumming, Jeff Ball, Junior Tremblay, Erin Bayne
Affiliation(s): Boreal Avian Modelling Project
Organization Type: eNGO

Abstract
The boreal forest is a vast and remote biome covering much of northern Canada and Alaska, which provides habitat for a substantial portion of the nearctic avifauna. The remoteness of much of this region limited earlier systematic monitoring efforts that hampered the development of data-informed decision support tools to aid bird conservation in response to increasing anthropogenic pressures. The Boreal Avian Modelling project (BAM) was formed as a collaborative initiative in 2004 to address this gap by amalgamating, harmonizing and integrating disparate point count data to develop tools for boreal bird conservation. We provide an overview of BAM’s development, including the individual research groups that make up the collective BAM team, and highlight BAM’s keystone tools for bird conservation. We discuss evolving conservation challenges and opportunities of new technologies and open data initiatives that can enhance science-based conservation tools for boreal birds.

Return to Program

title: Showcases
section: Warblers to Waterbirds: Where and Why?
text:
Warblers to Waterbirds: Where and Why?
Presenter(s): Tabatha Cormier (PhD Student), Nathanya Goudreau (MSc Student)
Affiliation(s): McGill University
Organization Type: Academic

Abstract
Our research group, based at McGill University’s Macdonald Campus, investigates the link between avian movement, contaminants, physiology, and life history across many scales. Our study regions range from the Neotropics (Belize) to the Arctic (Hudson Bay), and many places in-between (Gulf of Alaska, St Lawrence system, Island of Montreal). In long-term collaboration with the McGill Bird Observatory, we investigate urban habitat use of migratory and resident songbirds around Montreal. We are also part of two long-term seabird monitoring programs at Middleton and Coats islands, where we work to understand the impacts of anthropogenic pressures on northern marine ecosystems. To answer our research questions, we combine biologging techniques (GPS, geolocators, accelerometry) and emerging technologies (drones, uncrewed surface vehicles), along with physiological biomarkers, contaminants, and doubly-labelled water experiments. Overall, our research informs management decisions and deepens our collective understanding of the natural world.

title: Participate
text: Participants of the 2025 SCO-SOC Meeting in Saskatoon

title: Participate
section: Footnotes
text:
Footnotes


Between 10 and 15 minutes, including questions. Exact length will be determined when the final scientific program is developed.↩︎

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Description
WildTrax is a free online platform developed to remove the barrier of entry for researchers and managers to manage and process ARU recordings at any scale of research program. Developed to help users manage and process acoustic data from raw recordings through to being used in statistical analyses, WildTrax allows users to store raw data, metadata, and derived species data on a cloud system that can be shared by collaborators anywhere in the world. With standardized metadata formats, processing methods and report outputs, the data can be easily stored and shared between users.
In this workshop we’ll teach you how to upload and manage acoustic recordings; store and manage metadata such as survey locations, field site visits, and field equipment information; process the recordings to identify the species vocalizing in the recordings; create and manage projects to aggregate your data into collective reports; use the built in birdsong recognizers (BirdNET and HawkEars) to enhance your processing abilities; and publish, share and download the data contained on WildTrax.
The workshop is set-up to be interactive so that users can follow along on the WildTrax platform to perform the various steps being presented using their own data or example data provided by the facilitators to learn the various features and functions available in WildTrax.


Target audience
This workshop is meant to appeal to novice passive acoustic monitoring users (basic understanding of an ARU and spectrogram) to a more experienced researcher wanting to learn how WildTrax can help with their data storage and processing needs.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Basic understanding of passive acoustic recordings and spectrograms is helpful.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Description
Do you struggle to keep track of your research analyses in R? Is future-self constantly complaining about past-self’s inability to document scripts, data, and package versions? Come explore the magic of Quarto! Turn your R scripts into reproducible reports complete with all the information future-self needs to write up your analyses.
In this workshop we’ll learn about Markdown, RMarkdown and Quarto. We’ll cover different ways of creating reports from scripts and how to customize the output to maximize beauty as well as reproducibility. You will have the opportunity to learn and practice, and will go home with a collection of resources to help you along your journey. Example scripts to work with will be available, but best is to bring your own. Note that while the focus of this workshop is on R, Quarto also works with Python, so feel free to bring Python scripts to play with as well.
Participants do not need to be R experts but should be somewhat familiar with R and an R IDE (e.g., RStudio or Positron). Instructions for installing these programs will be sent out before the workshop.


Target audience
Anyone who has a passing familiarity with R/Python and who would like to learn more about creating reproducible reports of their analyses.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Some familiarity with R and an R IDE (e.g., RStudio or Positron). Basic familiarity with R or Python is recommended.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Description
Combine BirdNET Analyzer and Kaleidoscope Pro for robust acoustic analysis, quality assurance (QA), and custom recogniser development. We will work through two real-world workflows: (i) running automated detections for birds in BirdNET, and then using Kaleidoscope Pro for efficient manual QA, and (ii) applying Kaleidoscope’s clustering tools to generate high-quality training clips for building a custom amphibian BirdNET recognizer. Attendees will gain a clear introduction to how these two powerful software tools can be used together to leverage the strengths of each, creating an efficient and scalable workflow for acoustic monitoring. Designed for ecologists, bioacousticians, and practitioners, this Wildlife Acoustics workshop emphasizes practical skills, reproducible workflows, and confidence in automated acoustic monitoring results.


Target audience
Ecologists, bioacousticians, and practitioners.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
General knowledge of Kaleidoscope and BirdNET are helpful for this workshop.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Description
bbsBayes2 is an R-package that allows for easy access to the North American Breeding Bird Survey data and for fitting a selection of hierarchical Bayesian models that estimate status and trends and can be modified to estimate the influence of spatial and temporal predictors on trends and abundance (e.g., Mckellar et al. 2025 and Smith et al. 2024). The package provides efficient, fully-Bayesian parameter estimation using Stan, spatially explicit models well suited to exploring spatial patterns in trends, and options to customize models to include covariates of trends and abundance. The package also allows for a streamlined Bayesian workflow that facilitates convergence diagnostics, prior and posterior predictions, visualization of model predictions, as well as cross-validation and model comparisons.
During this workshop you will follow along with live coding to get hands-on experience manipulating and visualizing output from a range of models and species. We will demonstrate the package’s functions, including those that allow you to:

Prepare the data for modeling a given species and fit a model that estimates trends for any time period (1970-2019, 2005-2015, 1980-2000, etc.).

Generate heat maps of population trends and graph population trajectories.

Choose from a suite of built-in hierarchical Bayesian models and access tools to help with cross-validation and model comparison.

Estimate trends for customized regions (e.g., all of the eastern Boreal, Great Plains versus eastern populations of grassland birds).

Build and fit customized models: the workshop allows plenty of time to explore customizing the base-models with examples of models that estimate regional climate-effects on population trends, local landcover effects on mean abundance, landcover change effects on trends, etc.



Target audience
Researchers and students interested in:

modeling spatial and temporal patterns in bird population over the last 60 years;

modeling covariates or causal drivers of variations in abundance and trends;

gaining experience in Bayesian workflow and fitting models using Stan;

better understanding the models used by the Canadian Wildlife Service, and the United States Geological Survey to estimate status and trends from BBS data.



Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Familiarity with coding in R is necessary. No prior experience with Stan or Bayesian models is required.

title: Sponsors
text:
Position your brand as a leader in the field of bird science and conservation!
We invite you to partner with the Society of Canadian Ornithologists to advance scientific research and conservation efforts for birds while fostering the professional development of the ornithological community. By becoming a sponsor of the SCO-SOC 2026 virtual conference, you play a vital role in supporting this important initiative.

About the Conference
The Society of Canadian Ornithologists holds annual meetings, frequently in collaboration with other societies, to share scientific research and strengthen our conservation community. Our conferences feature a diverse array of workshops, plenary talks, oral and poster sessions, and symposia covering a wide range of topics. We also provide training and professional development opportunities for students and early-career professionals.
This year’s conference will be virtual, engaging a broader community of participants from across Canada and beyond. Our last virtual conference was a resounding success, engaging more than 175 participants, which we anticipate we will exceed in 2026. Approximately half of our attendees will hold advanced degrees and conduct bird-related research at universities, provincial and federal agencies, private industry, or environmental NGOs focused on conservation and the biology of wild birds. The other half will comprise amateur ornithologists and students, who will be the next generation of avian conservation practitioners in Canada.


Why Sponsor the SCO-SOC 2026 Virtual Conference?
The SCO-SOC 2026 Virtual Conference offers a unique platform to showcase cutting-edge science that is vital for biodiversity conservation in Canada. Here’s why you should consider becoming a sponsor:

Engage with a passionate online audience: Reach attendees who are actively looking for new tools, technologies, optics, field gear, and digital resources to enhance their research and conservation work.
Showcase your innovations: Present your products and services in a focused virtual environment to researchers, conservation professionals, and students who are seeking solutions they can apply immediately.
Build meaningful partnerships: Take advantage of interactive virtual networking spaces to connect with key stakeholders, potential collaborators, and future employees across the ornithological community.
Gain visibility and recognition: Your brand will be highlighted throughout the virtual venue and conference communications, ensuring prominent recognition among scientific and conservation leaders.
Make a lasting impact: Sponsorship directly supports ornithological research and conservation initiatives, helping to safeguard Canada’s avian biodiversity and promote a sustainable future for wild birds.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
The Institute for Wetland and Waterfowl Research (IWWR) at Ducks Unlimited Canada conducts interdisciplinary research that advances ornithology within the broader context of wetland ecosystems. Centered on waterfowl ecology, IWWR studies waterfowl habitat use, productivity, and population dynamics, with particular emphasis on breeding landscapes such as the Prairie Pothole Region, while extending across boreal and other wetland systems nationally. Beyond core ornithological questions, IWWR develops quantitative tools and models to guide conservation planning and evaluate program outcomes, ensuring that habitat investments are effective, adaptive, and evidence-based. This includes assessing when programs should be refined, scaled, or phased out. Complementing our work in waterfowl ecology, we have developed substantial research capacity in biodiversity, climate change, carbon dynamics, wetland biogeochemistry, and sustainable agriculture, reflecting emerging priorities in wetland conservation science. Together, these efforts integrate waterfowl science with broader ecological processes to support both understanding and adaptive, outcome-driven conservation.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
The Avian Behaviour and Conservation Lab at the University of Manitoba investigates how birds respond behaviourally to anthropogenic environmental change. Our research explores how birds time their daily and seasonal activities, and how flexibly these schedules respond to new conditions associated with climate change. We also investigate how artificial light at night alters the timing and movement behaviour of birds. Through field-based experiments, from the Neotropics to the sub-Arctic, we study primarily migratory species, including aerial insectivores such as Purple Martin and Eastern Whip-poor-will. We also collaborate with northern Indigenous communities in Manitoba to address shared concerns regarding change in avian behaviour and populations.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
The boreal forest is a vast and remote biome covering much of northern Canada and Alaska, which provides habitat for a substantial portion of the nearctic avifauna. The remoteness of much of this region limited earlier systematic monitoring efforts that hampered the development of data-informed decision support tools to aid bird conservation in response to increasing anthropogenic pressures. The Boreal Avian Modelling project (BAM) was formed as a collaborative initiative in 2004 to address this gap by amalgamating, harmonizing and integrating disparate point count data to develop tools for boreal bird conservation. We provide an overview of BAM’s development, including the individual research groups that make up the collective BAM team, and highlight BAM’s keystone tools for bird conservation. We discuss evolving conservation challenges and opportunities of new technologies and open data initiatives that can enhance science-based conservation tools for boreal birds.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
Birds Canada delivers strong science across the country and beyond through more than a dozen regional networks of employees, volunteers, and partners collaborating toward shared conservation goals. Our science is diverse involving many different research themes, core questions, methodologies, ecosystems, and focal species. Much of our science falls under two key initiatives: tracking the state of Canada’s birds and leveraging science to drive conservation. Our 50+ science staff members leverage data from volunteer scientists and conservation technologies (NatureCounts, Motus, WildTrax, and more) to answer timely questions of conservation interest, and we often partner with universities on graduate student projects. Ultimately all of our science helps generate the critical knowledge and evidence needed to catalyze actions that help birds and their habitats thrive. In this presentation, we give a top-down overview of science at Birds Canada, and describe a broad selection of examples of Birds Canada science in action.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
Drs Ann McKellar, Nancy Mahony, Greg Mitchell, and Elizabeth Gow are Research Scientists from Environment and Climate Change Canada’s Science and Technology Branch, Biodiversity Research Division, who study the conservation of migratory birds in working landscapes. Their research, which extends from the Canadian western forests and Prairies to Atlantic Canada, takes a full annual cycle approach to 1) examine effects of agriculture, forestry, and urbanization on avian distribution, abundance, and fitness, 2) assess population structure and migratory connectivity of priority species and species at risk, and 3) quantify threats associated with aerial and urban habitats. They aim to develop evidence-based solutions that support biodiversity, ecosystem goods and services, and human well-being in working landscapes. The team, including their graduate students, collaborates extensively with universities (where they are Adjunct professors), ENGOs, other federal departments, and governments, both nationally and internationally.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
Our research group, based at McGill University’s Macdonald Campus, investigates the link between avian movement, contaminants, physiology, and life history across many scales. Our study regions range from the Neotropics (Belize) to the Arctic (Hudson Bay), and many places in-between (Gulf of Alaska, St Lawrence system, Island of Montreal). In long-term collaboration with the McGill Bird Observatory, we investigate urban habitat use of migratory and resident songbirds around Montreal. We are also part of two long-term seabird monitoring programs at Middleton and Coats islands, where we work to understand the impacts of anthropogenic pressures on northern marine ecosystems. To answer our research questions, we combine biologging techniques (GPS, geolocators, accelerometry) and emerging technologies (drones, uncrewed surface vehicles), along with physiological biomarkers, contaminants, and doubly-labelled water experiments. Overall, our research informs management decisions and deepens our collective understanding of the natural world.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
At the Wildlife Research and Monitoring Section of the Ontario Ministry of Natural Resources, we coordinate three strands of avian research: the breeding ecology of shorebirds and waterfowl in the Hudson Bay Lowlands, the nutrient dynamics behind grassland bird habitat use, and climate effects on Wild Turkey distribution. Glen Brown’s work in Ontario’s Far North - one of the largest contiguous wetland systems in the world - has shown how the region may be vulnerable to the deepening of the permafrost active layer and wetland drying. Richard Feldman’s work in the Carden Alvar ecosystem has shown the connections between plant stoichiometry and variation in the quantity and quality of the prey of Eastern Whip-poor-will. Jeff Bowman’s work has focused on strategies that Ontario’s reintroduced Wild Turkeys have used to succeed in a northern climate. Combined, we contribute to conserving birds across the province, and involving students in applied ecological research.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
Thompson Rivers University is in the southern interior of British Columbia on the ancestral lands of the Tk’emlúps te Secwépemc (Kamloops main campus) within Secwepemcúl̓ecw, the traditional and unceded territory of the Secwépemc. This vast area of grasslands, forests, mountains, rivers and lakes is larger than all but four European countries, stretching from Jasper to Revelstoke to Kamloops to north of Williams Lake. Its diverse landscapes provide a rich opportunity for research about the impact of accelerating environmental change on avian ecology and to partner with First Nations. This showcase will highlight core themes of research guided by a group of faculty members who collaboratively mentor graduate students on projects that include: conservation of grassland-dependent bird species; braiding Indigenous and Western knowledges to inform species conservation; responses of bird communities to urbanization, industrial activity, and wildfire; and community-engaged efforts to monitor avian population change.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
We are an ecotoxicology lab with a strong conservation focus. Our lab studies the effects of environmental contaminants and other non-pollution stressors to better understand how individual birds, populations and communities respond to threats. We use a variety of methods from captive and field controlled dosing studies, beaker and limnocorral studies on aquatic insects that are important prey, and small and big scale field experiments to assess short and long term responses to communities across the Prairie region. A major focus is on how pesticides and other agricultural stressors impact common grassland and wetland associated songbirds through altered prey, neurotoxicity, and disturbance. We also conduct research on the effects of industrial pollution to shorebirds.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
Parks Canada Agency monitors birds in protected areas across Canada within a common framework called the Ecological Integrity Monitoring Program (EIMP). This program assesses the status and trend of ecological integrity of 119 ecosystems within 43 national parks across the country and can enable prioritization and evaluation of management actions to enhance ecosystem integrity. Birds are one of the most common indicators, being monitored within six different ecosystem types in 39 parks. Of the avian indicators assessed in 2025, the proportion in good, fair and poor condition was 56%, 31%, and 11%, and the proportion improving, stable and declining was 11%, 43% and 19%. We provide a brief overview of the EIMP and a summary of bird indicators within the program. We also present a case study from a protected area to demonstrate how a monitoring indicator can be developed and used in the ecosystem management planning process.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
The Canadian Network of Avian Data (CanAvian) unites a broad community of researchers, conservation practitioners, and volunteer scientists who generate, manage, and share extensive biodiversity data needed for ecological research. CanAvian is governed by a Steering Committee, Advisory Committees, and Working Groups coordinating priorities across science, data management, and community engagement. CanAvian brings together three of Canada’s largest biodiversity data platforms: NatureCounts (380 million records), Motus (8 billion records), and WildTrax (16 million records), supporting over 4,500 scientific publications. Supported by the Digital Research Alliance of Canada, CanAvian is establishing harmonized data access policies and interoperability standards to support FAIR and CARE principles and improve data sharing across platforms and data repositories. These connections will support increasingly integrated and large-scale analyses required by modern ecological research and Canada’s biodiversity commitments. CanAvian has open pathways for membership, data contribution, and data access and invites participation from across Canada’s ornithological community.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
The Advanced Facility for Avian Research at Western University is a world class facility consisting of faculty and trainees that aim to understand how the environment shapes bird ecology. Researchers across disciplines use integrative approaches to investigate everything bird, from neurobiology and behaviour to migratory physiology and conservation. To showcase the innovative research possible at AFAR, we will present on the tools and facilities used to address a multitude of research questions, both ongoing and for the future. In addition to working toward a better understanding of bird biology and ecology, we are passionate about training the next generation of critical thinkers and scientists.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
The Canadian Wildlife Service’s national wildlife management and assessment teams conduct research supporting the population monitoring and management of Canada’s migratory birds. Research centers around three overall themes: 1) optimizing applied population monitoring, for both professional and citizen science programs, as well as automated monitoring technologies; 2) developing integrated statistical methods to improve estimates of population sizes, status, trends and distribution; and 3) coordinating research and reporting with international partners to inform conservation across the full annual cycle. Ongoing and upcoming research projects involve: developing integrated spatiotemporal models of population status and drivers of trends using monitoring, remote sensing, and demographic data; developing object detection models to automate counts from seabird colony photo surveys; improving statistical models for estimating density and population sizes; improving breeding waterfowl population estimates by optimizing survey designs and accounting for bias in sex ratios; and improving surveys and models to estimate harvest of migratory birds.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
Partners In Flight (PIF) is a network of organizations dedicated to landbird conservation. Partners range from not-for-profit organizations to academics, governments, funders, and industry. Each partner brings their strengths, capacities, and expertise to achieve conservation for our shared birds. This presentation will highlight PIF’s science-based, open access data tools such as the Avian Conservation and Assessment Database, a species conservation status assessment tool that evaluates vulnerability factors at continental and regional scales. Other initiatives include revised model for population estimates, tools to evaluate progress in achieving continental population trend objectives and maps to explore migratory connections in the absence of tracking data. Finally, we’ll focus on how these tools support adaptive management and measurable outcomes for birds and how we (including you), as the bird research and conservation community can leverage and amplify these tools and approaches for bird conservation.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
Based at the University of New Brunswick’s Saint John Campus, the Atlantic Laboratory for Avian Research is directed by seabird biologist Dr. Heather Major. The lab studies relationships between bird populations and environmental change in marine and island ecosystems. Species of interest include the Atlantic Puffin, Razorbill, Common Murre, Arctic Tern, and Leach’s Storm-petrel primarily studied on Machias Seal Island (MSI) in the Bay of Fundy. MSI has been the focal site for the labs’ long-term seabird monitoring project that has been operated annually since 1995. In addition, the lab is currently collaborating on a project assessing feasibility of an island restoration at Seal Island, NS by removing introduced species.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Abstract
Banding and recapture of birds in the early 1900s provided the first documentation of individual bird movements in North America. In recent decades, bird monitoring has been revolutionized by technological advances such as satellite telemetry, geolocators, and automated radio telemetry (e.g., Motus), as well as the growth of ‘big data’ through participatory science, and the use of artificial intelligence for species identification. This has prompted questions about whether traditional methods such as banding have become irrelevant. On the contrary, standardized banding efforts remain fundamental to understanding longevity, annual survival rates, age structure, and physiological responses to environmental change. Banding data also support a wide range of conservation and management outcomes, including disease surveillance and assessment of habitat restoration. Since 1998, the Canadian Migration Monitoring Network has facilitated communication and collaboration among bird observatories across the country. Maintaining strong banding programs while integrating emerging technologies is critical to advancing ornithological research and conservation.

title: Resources
text: In progress…

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Description
Much of the research conducted on birds involves, either explicitly or implicitly, modelling patterns of habitat use in order to understand species ecology and the potential effects of land-use and climate change on populations. Patterns of habitat use by birds are the realization of the habitat selection process, which is governed by the interaction of individual species ecology and landscape ecological context. The purpose of this workshop is to provide a basic understanding of how habitat modelling techniques, such as occupancy modelling and resource selection functions, can be used to make valid inferences about species ecology and habitat selection.
In this workshop we will use a combination of interactive lectures and hands on work in R to:

Gain an understanding of basic principles of avian habitat ecology;

Understand habitat modelling theory in terms of habitat use, availability, preference, and selection; and

Understand how occupancy models and resource selection functions operate to provide inferences about occupancy patterns and drivers of habitat selection.

An important aspect of this workshop will be using simple data simulations to better understand the data generation process and how it interacts with the models to produce the results we see. This will allow participants to better understand and interpret model outputs in their ecological context Thus, the focus of this workshop will be less on how to run models, and more on using the models to gain reliable knowledge about important ecological processes. I will provide some example datasets, and participants are welcome to use their own data to work with the models.
Expected outcomes will be a better knowledge of avian habitat ecology and increased ability to understand and properly utilize common habitat modelling techniques for research and conservation.


Target audience
Students and professionals looking for a deeper understanding of habitat ecology and how modelling can be used to understand avian habitat selection.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Basic R skills and understanding of generalized linear models. Understanding for-loops will be particularly important. I will send preliminary material to familiarize participants with necessary concepts ahead of time.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Description
For migratory wildlife populations, understanding the connections between breeding, non-breeding, and stopover locations is integral to their conservation and management. Intrinsic markers, such as stable isotopes, found within the tissues of migratory individuals offer an alternative to the use of transmitters and other extrinsic markers. Briefly, this method relates the stable-isotope values of consumer tissues to those of the environment (especially stable isotopes of hydrogen and oxygen in precipitation) to determine likely origins where tissues were grown. In recent years there has been a handful of helpful packages released in the R statistical environment (isocat and AssignR) which have increased the functionality of these assignment methods and made them much more accessible.
In this workshop, I will first present a short introduction to the theory behind stable-isotope assignment, after which we will work through an example R script where we will, step-by-step, work though the procedures to determine the likely origins of unknown-origin migrants. No data is required by participants as everything will be provided during the workshop, but participants are welcome to apply the code to their own data.
The expected outcome for this workshop is that participants will gain a better understanding for the general approach and theory behind stable-isotope assignment using hydrogen isotopes and leave with some fundamental R code to build upon for their own analyses.


Target audience
Researchers and students who are interested in or are planning to collect tissues for stable isotope assignment. This workshop is aimed at a general audience.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Some familiarity with R/Rmarkdown is suggested.

title: SCO-SOC Virtual Meeting 2026
text:
Description
Despite the federal prohibition on killing migratory birds and destroying their nests, approximately 269 million birds are killed and almost one million nests are destroyed annually in Canada due to otherwise lawful human activities. Bird mortality results from window collisions, fisheries bycatch, powerline electrocution, among other causes. Nest destruction is primarily associated with vegetation management activities during the breeding season including both temporary activities such as agriculture or forestry and permanent land conversion such as urbanization. This unintentional (incidental) take includes almost all spheres of human activities, both industrial and non-industrial, and is expected to increase as human development accelerates.
In the last few years, there has been renewed interest in regulating incidental take at the federal level. A regulatory system could provide benefits to migratory birds through the universal adoption of beneficial management practices in exchange for more legal certainty. However, such an initiative requires a strong scientific foundation to ensure that decision-making achieves conservation goals. For example, understanding the scope and impact of an activity is essential for establishing a baseline against which mitigation measures can be evaluated. Further research is also needed to evaluate evidence-based mitigation measures and their applicability in the Canadian context.
This workshop will examine the concept of incidental take and will aim to foster discussion on how scientists can influence regulatory and policy development through their research. The workshop will include a presentation followed by an open discussion with participants. The expected outcome is that participants will gain a better understanding of how to present the results of their research to policymakers to better support policy development for migratory birds in Canada. The workshop may also help researchers incorporate incidental take considerations into their work and strengthen collaboration between scientists and policymakers.


Target audience
The target audience consists of researchers with an interest in policy-making and with knowledge on the effects of human activities on migratory birds.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Some prior knowledge of the prohibitions of the Migratory Birds Convention Act and the Migratory Birds Regulations 2022 is useful.

title: Workshops
text:
Lead: Carlos Abrahams
Length: 2-3 hours


Combine BirdNET Analyzer and Kaleidoscope Pro for robust acoustic analysis, quality assurance (QA), and custom recogniser development. We will work through two real-world workflows: (i) running automated detections for birds in BirdNET, and then using Kaleidoscope Pro for efficient manual QA, and (ii) applying Kaleidoscope’s clustering tools to generate high-quality training clips for building a custom amphibian BirdNET recognizer. Attendees will gain a clear introduction to how these two powerful software tools can be used together to leverage the strengths of each, creating an efficient and scalable workflow for acoustic monitoring. Designed for ecologists, bioacousticians, and practitioners, this Wildlife Acoustics workshop emphasizes practical skills, reproducible workflows, and confidence in automated acoustic monitoring results.



Ecologists, bioacousticians, and practitioners.



General knowledge of Kaleidoscope and BirdNET are helpful for this workshop.

Return to Program

title: Workshops
text:

Combine BirdNET Analyzer and Kaleidoscope Pro for robust acoustic analysis, quality assurance (QA), and custom recogniser development. title: Workshops
section: Introduction to Quarto for Reproducibility
text:
Introduction to Quarto for Reproducibility
Lead: Steffi LaZerte
Length: 2-2.5 hours

Description
Do you struggle to keep track of your research analyses in R? Is future-self constantly complaining about past-self’s inability to document scripts, data, and package versions? Come explore the magic of Quarto! Turn your R scripts into reproducible reports complete with all the information future-self needs to write up your analyses.
In this workshop we’ll learn about Markdown, RMarkdown and Quarto. We’ll cover different ways of creating reports from scripts and how to customize the output to maximize beauty as well as reproducibility. You will have the opportunity to learn and practice, and will go home with a collection of resources to help you along your journey. Example scripts to work with will be available, but best is to bring your own. Note that while the focus of this workshop is on R, Quarto also works with Python, so feel free to bring Python scripts to play with as well.
Participants do not need to be R experts but should be somewhat familiar with R and an R IDE (e.g., RStudio or Positron). Instructions for installing these programs will be sent out before the workshop.


Target audience
Anyone who has a passing familiarity with R/Python and who would like to learn more about creating reproducible reports of their analyses.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Some familiarity with R and an R IDE (e.g., RStudio or Positron). Basic familiarity with R or Python is recommended.

Return to Program

title: Workshops
section: Introduction to stable-isotope assignment in R
text:
Introduction to stable-isotope assignment in R
Lead: Jackson Kusack
Length: 2-2.5 hours

Description
For migratory wildlife populations, understanding the connections between breeding, non-breeding, and stopover locations is integral to their conservation and management. Intrinsic markers, such as stable isotopes, found within the tissues of migratory individuals offer an alternative to the use of transmitters and other extrinsic markers. Briefly, this method relates the stable-isotope values of consumer tissues to those of the environment (especially stable isotopes of hydrogen and oxygen in precipitation) to determine likely origins where tissues were grown. In recent years there has been a handful of helpful packages released in the R statistical environment (isocat and AssignR) which have increased the functionality of these assignment methods and made them much more accessible.
In this workshop, I will first present a short introduction to the theory behind stable-isotope assignment, after which we will work through an example R script where we will, step-by-step, work though the procedures to determine the likely origins of unknown-origin migrants. No data is required by participants as everything will be provided during the workshop, but participants are welcome to apply the code to their own data.
The expected outcome for this workshop is that participants will gain a better understanding for the general approach and theory behind stable-isotope assignment using hydrogen isotopes and leave with some fundamental R code to build upon for their own analyses.


Target audience
Researchers and students who are interested in or are planning to collect tissues for stable isotope assignment. This workshop is aimed at a general audience.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Some familiarity with R/Rmarkdown is suggested.

Return to Program

title: Workshops
section: Modeling Breeding Bird Survey with bbsBayes2: status, trends, and causal modeling of drivers of trends
text:
Modeling Breeding Bird Survey with bbsBayes2: status, trends, and causal modeling of drivers of trends
Lead: Adam Smith
Length: 2-2.5 hours

Description
bbsBayes2 is an R-package that allows for easy access to the North American Breeding Bird Survey data and for fitting a selection of hierarchical Bayesian models that estimate status and trends and can be modified to estimate the influence of spatial and temporal predictors on trends and abundance (e.g., Mckellar et al. 2025 and Smith et al. 2024). The package provides efficient, fully-Bayesian parameter estimation using Stan, spatially explicit models well suited to exploring spatial patterns in trends, and options to customize models to include covariates of trends and abundance. The package also allows for a streamlined Bayesian workflow that facilitates convergence diagnostics, prior and posterior predictions, visualization of model predictions, as well as cross-validation and model comparisons.
During this workshop you will follow along with live coding to get hands-on experience manipulating and visualizing output from a range of models and species. We will demonstrate the package’s functions, including those that allow you to:

Prepare the data for modeling a given species and fit a model that estimates trends for any time period (1970-2019, 2005-2015, 1980-2000, etc.).

Generate heat maps of population trends and graph population trajectories.

Choose from a suite of built-in hierarchical Bayesian models and access tools to help with cross-validation and model comparison.

Estimate trends for customized regions (e.g., all of the eastern Boreal, Great Plains versus eastern populations of grassland birds).

Build and fit customized models: the workshop allows plenty of time to explore customizing the base-models with examples of models that estimate regional climate-effects on population trends, local landcover effects on mean abundance, landcover change effects on trends, etc.



Target audience
Researchers and students interested in:

modeling spatial and temporal patterns in bird population over the last 60 years;

modeling covariates or causal drivers of variations in abundance and trends;

gaining experience in Bayesian workflow and fitting models using Stan;

better understanding the models used by the Canadian Wildlife Service, and the United States Geological Survey to estimate status and trends from BBS data.



Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Familiarity with coding in R is necessary. No prior experience with Stan or Bayesian models is required.

Return to Program

title: Workshops
section: Modelling Avian Habitat Ecology
text:
Modelling Avian Habitat Ecology
Lead: Andy Crosby
Length: Full day / 4-5 hours

Description
Much of the research conducted on birds involves, either explicitly or implicitly, modelling patterns of habitat use in order to understand species ecology and the potential effects of land-use and climate change on populations. Patterns of habitat use by birds are the realization of the habitat selection process, which is governed by the interaction of individual species ecology and landscape ecological context. The purpose of this workshop is to provide a basic understanding of how habitat modelling techniques, such as occupancy modelling and resource selection functions, can be used to make valid inferences about species ecology and habitat selection.

Understand how occupancy models and resource selection functions operate to provide inferences about occupancy patterns and drivers of habitat selection.

An important aspect of this workshop will be using simple data simulations to better understand the data generation process and how it interacts with the models to produce the results we see. This will allow participants to better understand and interpret model outputs in their ecological context Thus, the focus of this workshop will be less on how to run models, and more on using the models to gain reliable knowledge about important ecological processes. I will provide some example datasets, and participants are welcome to use their own data to work with the models.
Expected outcomes will be a better knowledge of avian habitat ecology and increased ability to understand and properly utilize common habitat modelling techniques for research and conservation.


Target audience
Students and professionals looking for a deeper understanding of habitat ecology and how modelling can be used to understand avian habitat selection.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Basic R skills and understanding of generalized linear models. Understanding for-loops will be particularly important. I will send preliminary material to familiarize participants with necessary concepts ahead of time.

title: Workshops
section: Regulating incidental take in Canada: challenges and opportunities for scientists
text:
Regulating incidental take in Canada: challenges and opportunities for scientists
Lead: Julie Bourque
Length: 2-2.5 hours

Description
Despite the federal prohibition on killing migratory birds and destroying their nests, approximately 269 million birds are killed and almost one million nests are destroyed annually in Canada due to otherwise lawful human activities. Bird mortality results from window collisions, fisheries bycatch, powerline electrocution, among other causes. Nest destruction is primarily associated with vegetation management activities during the breeding season including both temporary activities such as agriculture or forestry and permanent land conversion such as urbanization. This unintentional (incidental) take includes almost all spheres of human activities, both industrial and non-industrial, and is expected to increase as human development accelerates.
In the last few years, there has been renewed interest in regulating incidental take at the federal level. A regulatory system could provide benefits to migratory birds through the universal adoption of beneficial management practices in exchange for more legal certainty. However, such an initiative requires a strong scientific foundation to ensure that decision-making achieves conservation goals. For example, understanding the scope and impact of an activity is essential for establishing a baseline against which mitigation measures can be evaluated. Further research is also needed to evaluate evidence-based mitigation measures and their applicability in the Canadian context.
This workshop will examine the concept of incidental take and will aim to foster discussion on how scientists can influence regulatory and policy development through their research. The workshop will include a presentation followed by an open discussion with participants. The expected outcome is that participants will gain a better understanding of how to present the results of their research to policymakers to better support policy development for migratory birds in Canada. The workshop may also help researchers incorporate incidental take considerations into their work and strengthen collaboration between scientists and policymakers.


Target audience
The target audience consists of researchers with an interest in policy-making and with knowledge on the effects of human activities on migratory birds.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Some prior knowledge of the prohibitions of the Migratory Birds Convention Act and the Migratory Birds Regulations 2022 is useful.

Return to Program

title: Workshops
section: Using WildTrax to manage and process your birdsong recordings
text:
Using WildTrax to manage and process your birdsong recordings
Lead: Kevin Kelly
Length: Full day / 4-5 hours

Description
WildTrax is a free online platform developed to remove the barrier of entry for researchers and managers to manage and process ARU recordings at any scale of research program. Developed to help users manage and process acoustic data from raw recordings through to being used in statistical analyses, WildTrax allows users to store raw data, metadata, and derived species data on a cloud system that can be shared by collaborators anywhere in the world. With standardized metadata formats, processing methods and report outputs, the data can be easily stored and shared between users.
In this workshop we’ll teach you how to upload and manage acoustic recordings; store and manage metadata such as survey locations, field site visits, and field equipment information; process the recordings to identify the species vocalizing in the recordings; create and manage projects to aggregate your data into collective reports; use the built in birdsong recognizers (BirdNET and HawkEars) to enhance your processing abilities; and publish, share and download the data contained on WildTrax.
The workshop is set-up to be interactive so that users can follow along on the WildTrax platform to perform the various steps being presented using their own data or example data provided by the facilitators to learn the various features and functions available in WildTrax.


Target audience
This workshop is meant to appeal to novice passive acoustic monitoring users (basic understanding of an ARU and spectrogram) to a more experienced researcher wanting to learn how WildTrax can help with their data storage and processing needs.


Prerequisite skills/knowledge
Basic understanding of passive acoustic recordings and spectrograms is helpful.

title: Meeting Code of Conduct
text:
This Code of Conduct is intended to set clear expectations of behavior at the 2026 meeting of the SCO-SOC to ensure that all participants feel safe and welcome and that SCO-SOC 2026 is a positive experience for all.
The SCO-SOC welcomes everyone and celebrates diversity in all its forms. The SCO-SOC is dedicated to providing a collaborative, supportive, and engaging environment for everyone involved and that is free of discrimination, harassment, and retaliation. We expect all participants to help foster a respectful and considerate environment that provides constructive critique, debate, and interactions that respectfully allow for differences of opinion while embracing engaging discussion.

Meeting Scope
We intend for SCO-SOC 2026 to foster open, honest and engaging communication of ornithological science. In so doing, we aim to promote equal opportunity and treatment for all participants. To achieve effective communication, we require attendees to conduct themselves in a courteous fashion and respect the intellectual property of our colleagues. We represent the field of ornithology, and it is imperative that we behave as professionals toward all conference participants, including, but not limited to, conference delegates, Council members, conference volunteers, sponsors, and exhibitors.
All forms of communication among conference participants during the meeting are considered within the scope of SCO-SOC 2026 and therefore fall under the Code of Conduct. These include public and private communications, social media posts, direct messages, phone calls, and other forms of written, verbal, and non-verbal (including visual) communication. Additionally, communication as described above occurring before or after the SCO-SOC 2026 meeting but pertaining to content associated with the meeting and/or relies on contact information of a meeting participant was procured before or during the meeting may also fall under the purview of this Code of Conduct.


Expectations of Participants
Harassment in any form between participants of SCO-SOC 2026 will not be tolerated. Harassment includes any communication with, or behavior towards another participant involving offensive gestures, verbal and written comments, posts on the internet and social media, deliberate intimidation, stalking, harassing photography or recording, sustained disruption of talks or other events, inappropriate physical or electronic contact, microaggressions, and unwelcome attention. Note that it is also possible to unintentionally engage in harassment and participants must be mindful of their behavior. Participants asked to stop any harassing behavior by anyone are expected to comply immediately, regardless of their intent.


Use of Media Associated With the Meeting
By registering for and attending this Meeting, Participants agree to allow SCO-SOC 2026 to use photos, videos, or audio recordings taken of them during the Meeting in publications, promotions, and on the websites and social media pages of the participating societies. Only the Society may (a) take/make or distribute audio or video recordings of the Meeting or Participants or (b) distribute them (via social media or other means).
Participants wishing to share information from the Event and/or post photos via social media, whether of their own material or that of others, must adhere to individual social media platforms’ stated rules and policies. We ask that presenters and hosts of Events that do not want content or information shared via social media clearly communicate this to members of the audience and that members of the audience adhere to such requests.


Reporting an Incident
Any individual covered by the Code of Conduct who believes that they have been subjected to harassment, notices that someone else is being harassed, or has any other concerns about an individual’s behavior should report the incident immediately. Similarly, any breaches of the Code associated with media use should also be reported immediately. Potential violations of the Meeting Code of Conduct should be reported promptly to the SCO-SOC 2026 Safe Meeting Committee via email at president@sco-soc.ca. The Committee will strive to acknowledge all reports within one hour of receipt (between 9am to 5pm). All complaints will be taken seriously, promptly reviewed, and may be subject to investigation. Reports should be submitted using the group email, which will be directed to the entire Committee. All information related to the complaint and any subsequent investigation will be treated with the utmost confidentiality and shared only with Committee members as necessary to ensure a thorough and fair review.


Disciplinary Action
Individuals found to have engaged in behavior prohibited by this policy, as well as those making bad faith allegations under the Code of Conduct will be subject to disciplinary action. The SCO-SOC 2026 Safe Meeting Committee may take any action they deem appropriate, ranging from a verbal warning or ejection/prohibition from the specific activity in question (e.g., workshop, symposium, or even the entire conference), banning them from future conferences run or jointly run by the partner societies, revoking membership in from the Society, and/or the reporting of their behavior to their employer and research funders.


Retaliation Is Prohibited
Any form of retaliation—including seeking to violate the confidentiality of a reporting individual—against individuals who file a complaint or assist in an investigation or against any individuals suspected of doing so will not be tolerated by the SCO-SOC 2026 meeting. Retaliation is a serious violation of this policy and like any breach of the Code itself, will be subject to disciplinary action.


Questions & Appeal
Any questions regarding this policy should be directed to the SCO-SOC 2026 Safe Meeting Committee. If an individual involved in any reported incident is dissatisfied with the disciplinary action, they may appeal to a committee made of the collective members of the executive committee from the Society.


Social Media Policy
The 2026 meeting of the SCO-SOC supports the use of social media; we encourage thoughtful and considerate sharing of conference content. In our view, sharing ornithological science with the public via social media is one tool for public education and outreach and a key part of gaining long-term support for science and conservation.
We encourage presenters to include personal and/or project social media handles, project hashtags or QR codes to external content on their slides (at least the opening/title slide, but recommended to use on all slides) and posters. For those without personal/project handles or hashtags, consider including the conference hashtag (#SCOSOC2026) or a social media icon on slides to let people know you are supportive of them posting about your work on social media.
Any presenters not wanting their presentation or research to be featured in social media posts must explicitly opt out of social media: this is best achieved by placing a “no social media” symbol on slides or materials. If presenters do not opt out in this manner, it shall be implied that conference organizers and/or attendees can share published research presented at the conference without consent.
Anyone may participate in conversations on the host societies’ social media channels; however, please be aware that:

Credit must be given to presenters/researchers whose presentation and/or research are featured in your post.
The host society has the right to delete posts or comments containing spam, irrelevant remarks, or items that do not align with their mission/integrity.
Personal attacks, promotion of violence or illegal activities, offensive material, or profanity will be deleted.

The host society reserves the right to ban users who violate this policy.
Conference attendees are expected to abide by the meeting code of conduct (above), and all those engaging on social media are asked to maintain apply the same respectful, courteous, inclusive, and supportive conduct online as in person to ensure a safe, hospitable, and productive conference. Discrimination, harassment, and/or retaliation will not be tolerated.
Additionally, we recommended that presenters and those sharing on social media be mindful of how photos of birds (and other animals) and their nests may appear to the public: Are safe and taxonomically appropriate grips being used? Does the bird appear in any way to be stressed, disheveled, or in an unnatural position? For suggestions on sharing photos of birds in the hand, see guidelines provided by the North American Banding Council. In addition, ask yourself if the use of a nest photo is helping to demonstrate something regarding your methods or results rather than just providing an interesting backdrop. For further guidelines on ethical photography of birds and their nests, see Audubon’s Guide to Ethical Bird Photography and Videography.

title: Virtual Meeting

October 5-7, 2026

Closed




We’re thrilled to announce that our 2026 Conference will take place online—opening the doors to a broad, diverse community of participants from across the globe! This year’s theme, Spreading Our Wings, celebrates accessibility, connection, and the joy of shared discovery within our research community. We can’t wait to engage with our members and explore new ways to learn and collaborate together.
Save the dates!
